title: How to Use Intuition When Making Spiritual Decisions
meta_description: Trust your gut to guide spiritual choices. This article shows how to stop doubting your inner knowing and make decisions with clarity.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Spiritual Decisions
og_description: Trust your gut to guide spiritual choices. This article shows how to stop doubting your inner knowing and make decisions with clarity.
body:
You Already Know More Than You Think

Let me be straight with you about something. You've been making spiritual decisions your whole life - you just didn't know that's what you were doing. Every time you chose to walk away from a conversation that felt heavy, every time you picked up a book that seemed to call your name, every time you felt a "no" in your gut but couldn't explain why - that was your intuition doing its job. The problem isn't that you don't have it. The problem is you've been trained to doubt it.

I talk to people every week who are frozen when it comes to spiritual decisions. Should I leave this church? Should I stop meditating? Should I take that healing course? Should I cut ties with this person who drains me? They want a sign. They want a clear message from the universe. They want someone else to tell them what to do. And I get it. That feels safer, doesn't it? Right?! You can blame someone else if it doesn't work out.

But here's the thing - the universe already gave you the equipment you need. That sinking feeling in your stomach? That's not indigestion. That's your soul saying "no." That electric buzz in your chest when you think about a certain path? That's your soul saying "yes." You don't need more signs. You need to start trusting the ones you're already getting.

Why Your Intuition Gets Muffled (And It's Not Your Fault)

Before we get into the how-to part, we have to talk about why this is so hard for you. Because it's not because you're broken. It's not because you're not spiritual enough. It's because you've been conditioned to override your own knowing.

Think about it. When you were a kid, you probably had a moment where you knew something was off about a person or a situation. Maybe you said something about it. And what happened? Someone told you not to be silly. Someone told you you were being too sensitive. Someone told you to stop making things up. Over and over, that message got drilled in: your inner knowing is wrong. Don't trust it.

That creates what I call the "bright wound" - that place where your sensitivity got hurt so badly that you learned to shut it down. You learned to second-guess yourself. You learned to look outside for answers because looking inside got you in trouble. Does that land? Because I see it in almost every person I work with.

So now when you have a spiritual decision to make, your mind kicks into overdrive. You make pros and cons lists. You ask five different people for advice. You google "signs from the universe" for three hours. You're trying to find your way back to that intuitive knowing, but you're taking the longest possible route.

The First Rule of Intuitive Decision-Making

Here's the rule I want you to try on: Your first reaction is usually right. Your second reaction is usually fear.

Think about the last big spiritual decision you faced. Remember that split-second feeling you had when the option first presented itself? That flash of "yes" or "no" that came before your brain had a chance to jump in? That was your intuition. And then what happened? Your brain said "but what if..." and you were off to the races.

Your intuition doesn't argue. It doesn't explain. It just knows. It's that simple, quiet knowing that doesn't need to prove itself. Your mind, on the other hand, loves to build cases. It loves to consider every possible outcome. It loves to keep you safe by keeping you stuck in indecision. Know what I mean?

So when you're facing a spiritual decision, here's what I want you to do: Notice your first reaction. Write it down if you have to. Then let your mind have its say. Let it list all the fears, all the what-ifs, all the reasons why you should wait or do the opposite. But don't let that second reaction override the first one. The first one came from your soul. The second one came from your survival instincts. They're not the same thing.

Your intuition doesn't argue. It doesn't explain. It just knows. That simple, quiet knowing that doesn't need to prove itself - that's the voice you've been looking for.

How to Tell the Difference Between Intuition and Fear

This is the million-dollar question, isn't it? How do you know if that feeling in your gut is genuine guidance or just anxiety dressed up as spirituality? I get asked this constantly, and here's the honest answer: it takes practice, but there are some reliable markers.

Intuition feels neutral. It doesn't have a charge. It's not excited or scared. It's just a knowing. Fear, on the other hand, feels urgent. It feels tight. It comes with a story. Fear says "if you do this, this terrible thing will happen." Intuition says "this is the way" and doesn't need to explain further.

Intuition is consistent. It doesn't change based on who you're talking to or what time of day it is. Fear fluctuates. You might feel one way in the morning and completely different by evening. That's fear bouncing around. Intuition stays steady.

Intuition feels like coming home. Even if the decision is hard - even if it means ending a relationship or leaving a job or changing your spiritual path - there's a sense of rightness underneath it. Fear feels like running away. Intuition feels like walking toward something true, even if it's uncomfortable.

I want you to practice noticing the difference this week. Pick one small spiritual decision - something low-stakes, like what book to read or what meditation to try. Notice how your first reaction feels. Then notice how your second reaction feels. Start building that muscle of discernment.

If you want a tool to help you work through this process, I've found See on Amazon (paid link) to be a solid resource for understanding how to separate genuine intuitive hits from fear-based thinking.

Making Decisions When You Feel Pulled in Two Directions

Sometimes the spiritual decision isn't between a clear yes and a clear no. Sometimes you're standing at a crossroads and both paths seem valid. Both have good reasons behind them. Both feel right in different ways. What do you do then?

First, stop trying to figure it out with your mind alone. You've been trying that and it's not working. You need to drop into your body. Your body holds wisdom that your brain can't access. Your brain is good at logic. Your body is good at truth.

Here's an exercise I give to people who are stuck between two spiritual choices. Close your eyes and imagine yourself choosing option A. Don't think about it - just imagine it. Feel yourself in that reality. Notice what happens in your body. Does your chest feel open or tight? Does your stomach relax or clench? Do your shoulders drop or rise? Now do the same with option B. Compare how your body responded. The option that made your body feel more expansive, more relaxed, more alive - that's your answer.

I'm not saying the easier path is always the right one. Sometimes the right decision feels heavy because it involves growth. But there's a difference between the heaviness of growth and the tightness of going against your truth. Growth heaviness feels like a deep breath before a big lift. Going-against-your-truth tightness feels like someone's hand around your throat.

What to Do When You Don't Get a Clear Answer

Let's be real. Sometimes you ask for guidance and all you get is silence. You sit in meditation, you light candles, you pray, you journal - and nothing. Crickets. That's frustrating, I know. But silence isn't a lack of guidance. Silence is guidance too.

When you don't get a clear answer, it usually means one of three things:

  • You're not ready yet. There's something you need to learn or experience before you can make this decision. The universe is asking you to wait, not because it's punishing you, but because the timing isn't right.
  • The answer doesn't matter as much as you think. Sometimes both options lead to the same place. The universe is saying "pick whichever one, it's all going to work out." That can be hard to hear when you want a dramatic sign, but it's actually freeing.
  • You're too in your head to hear the answer. The signal is there, but your mental noise is drowning it out. You need to quiet down before you can receive.

If you're in the third category, I recommend creating space for silence. Not more meditation apps. Not more YouTube videos about spiritual decisions. Just silence. Go for a walk without headphones. Sit in your car for five minutes without turning on the radio. Let your mind settle. The answer is often waiting in the spaces between your thoughts.

For those times when you need a more structured approach to clearing the mental noise, See on Amazon (paid link) offers practical techniques for creating that inner quiet.

The Danger of Spiritual Bypassing

I need to say something that might ruffle some feathers. Using your intuition to make spiritual decisions doesn't mean you get to skip the hard work. It doesn't mean you use "I'm following my intuition" as an excuse to avoid responsibility or to hurt other people.

I've seen people do this. Someone gets a "download from the universe" that they should leave their partner, so they ghost them. Someone gets a "sign" that they should drop out of their commitments, so they leave people hanging. That's not intuition. That's spiritual bypassing - using spiritual language to avoid being a decent human being.

Real intuition doesn't ask you to be irresponsible. Real intuition might ask you to have hard conversations. It might ask you to sit with discomfort. It might ask you to apologize or to show up when you'd rather run away. If your "intuition" is telling you to do something that goes against basic kindness and integrity, question it. Hard.

Your intuition will never tell you to harm yourself or others. It will never tell you to abandon your responsibilities without a clear and compassionate way to transition. If the guidance you're receiving feels selfish or cruel, it's not intuition. It's your ego or your fear dressed up in spiritual clothes.

Building Trust With Your Intuition Over Time

You can't go from doubting everything to making major life decisions based on a feeling overnight. That's not how trust works. Trust is built one small win at a time.

Start with tiny decisions. Which route should I take to work? What should I eat for lunch? Should I call this person today or wait? Make a choice based on your gut, and then notice what happens. When your choice leads to something good - a smooth drive, a delicious meal, a meaningful conversation - acknowledge that. Say "I listened to my intuition and it worked."

When your choice doesn't work out - you take the wrong route and get stuck in traffic, you pick a meal that doesn't satisfy you - don't use that as proof that your intuition is broken. Use it as data. What was different about that decision? Were you rushed? Were you anxious? Were you trying to force an answer instead of waiting for a clear one?

Over time, you'll develop a relationship with your intuition. You'll learn its voice. You'll learn when it speaks clearly and when it's being drowned out by other noise. You'll learn to trust it even when it doesn't make logical sense.

I've seen people make some of the most beautiful spiritual decisions of their lives by trusting this process. People who left careers that were killing their souls. People who moved to places they'd never been because it "felt right." People who ended relationships that looked good on paper but felt dead inside. Every single one of them will tell you the same thing: the intuitive choice was hard, but it was right.

For a deeper exploration of how to develop that trust in your own inner guidance, See on Amazon (paid link) is worth your time.

When to Take Action and When to Wait

One of the trickiest parts of intuitive decision-making is knowing when to act and when to sit still. Some spiritual teachers tell you to "trust the timing of the universe" to the point where you never take any action. Others tell you to "claim what you want" to the point where you're forcing doors that aren't meant to open.

Here's how I think about it. Your intuition will give you two kinds of guidance: the kind that says "go" and the kind that says "wait." The "go" guidance comes with energy. It feels like a push. It feels like momentum. You might feel excited or nervous, but there's a forward motion to it. The "wait" guidance feels like stillness. It feels like being held back. It's not a no - it's a not yet.

If you're getting "go" energy, take action. Even if you're scared. Even if you don't have all the answers. Take one step and trust that the next step will become clear when you need it. If you're getting "wait" energy, stop pushing. Stop trying to make something happen. Use that time to prepare, to heal, to learn what you need to learn. When the waiting period is over, the "go" energy will return.

The mistake most people make is trying to turn "wait" into "go" through effort and force. They think if they just try harder, the door will open. But some doors are locked for a reason. If you're forcing something that isn't moving, step back. Ask yourself if there's a different path you're not seeing.

Your Intuition Is Not a Magic 8-Ball

I want to be clear about something. Your intuition is not a tool for predicting the future. It's not a way to avoid pain or to guarantee that every decision works out perfectly. Your intuition is a compass, not a map. It points you in the right direction, but it doesn't show you every obstacle on the path.

You might make an intuitive decision that leads to a hard season. That doesn't mean you made the wrong choice. It means the right choice sometimes comes with challenges. The difference is this: when you make an intuitive choice, you have the energy and resilience to handle those challenges. When you make a fear-based choice, the challenges feel crushing because you're not aligned.

So stop expecting your intuition to make your life easy. It won't. What it will do is make your life true. It will guide you toward your authentic path, even when that path is steep. It will help you make decisions that honor your soul, even when those decisions cost you something.

And that's worth more than any easy answer. A life lived from your own deep knowing is a life that feels like yours. Not your parents' life. Not your culture's life. Not your church's life. Yours.

If you're ready to deepen your relationship with your own inner guidance, See on Amazon (paid link) offers a grounded approach to intuitive living that I keep coming back to.

You already have what you need. The answers are already inside you. The question isn't "how do I get guidance?" The question is "am I willing to trust the guidance I'm already receiving?" That's the real spiritual decision. And it's one only you can make.

If you're struggling to trust yourself and want more support in making intuitive spiritual decisions, take our free assessment at intuitivesawaken.com/assessment to see where you're blocked and what your next step is. The answers are closer than you think.
